html:
Daniel Carlsson Solander, born on February 19, 1733, in the parish of Piteå, and died on May 13, 1782, in London, was a Swedish botanist, naturalist, natural historian, explorer, and student of Carl von Linné.

**Early Life and Education**

Daniel Solander was born in the old town of Piteå in 1733. He was the son of the provost and member of parliament, Carl Solander, and Magdalena Bostadia, and remained unmarried throughout his life. At the age of seventeen, he came to Uppsala and became one of Linné's most prominent students. In 1753, he undertook a botanical expedition in the Pite Lappmark region, and likely also in the Torne Lappmark region in 1755; the collections from these expeditions are now held at the Swedish National Museum and the Bergius Botanical Garden.

**England**

In 1759, Linné sent him to England, where he arrived in 1760, delayed by illness. Linné suggested that he be appointed as a professor in St. Petersburg, but he declined. In 1763, he was appointed as an assistant librarian at the British Museum.

**Circumnavigation of the World**

Through his acquaintance with Joseph Banks, he participated in James Cook's circumnavigation of the world aboard the HMS Endeavour, which for the first time mapped and explored New Zealand and Australia, regions that were very poorly known at the time. The bay of Botany Bay, south of Sydney, as well as the headland Point Solander, were named after him due to the botanical discoveries he made there during the landing in 1770. The island group known as the Solander Islands was also named after him. In 1772, he traveled with Banks and Uno von Troil (later Archbishop) on a trip to Iceland.

**British Museum**

From 1773, Solander worked as a curator at the British Museum. In 1764, he was elected a member of the Royal Society, and in 1771 and around 1763, he was appointed an honorary doctor at the University of Oxford in law and medicine, respectively. In 1773, he was elected as foreign member number 86 of the Swedish Academy of Sciences.

== Legacy ==

During the voyage with Captain James Cook and botanist Joseph Banks aboard HMS Endeavour, Daniel Solander became the first Swede, and indeed the first person with a university education, to set foot in Australia. Daniel Solander is considered one of England's foremost authorities on natural history. He has been honored by having places around the world named after him. For example, in England, Australia (Cape Solander, Botany Bay), Canada (Solander Island in British Columbia), and New Zealand (the Solander Islands south of the South Island). To honor the memory of Daniel Solander, the Solander Society was founded in 1999. In his hometown of Piteå, there is Solandergården (Solander Farm) and Solanderparken (Solander Park), and in 2007, a science park named SolanderScience Park was inaugurated in his honor. There is also a school in Öjebyn called Solanderskolan, and a hiking trail between Piteå and the neighboring city of Luleå called Solanderleden. In 2001, commemorative postage stamps were issued as a joint venture between Sweden and Australia to celebrate Solander's botanical discoveries in Australia. In Hammarbyhöjden, Stockholm, Solandergatan (Solander Street) is named after him.

The plant *Banksia solandri* and the asteroid 13788 Dansolander are named in his honor. The author abbreviation "Sol." is used for Daniel Solander when referring to a scientific name in botany. The geranium species *Geranium solanderi* is also named after him.

Solander invented the Solander box, which is used in libraries and archives to store printed materials, documents, books, etc., in an archival-safe manner. Solander designed the box while working at the British Museum, cataloging the natural history collection. The box is typically made of sturdy cardboard or wood and has a lid to protect its contents.

== References ==

== Sources == Australian Dictionary of Biography: Solander, Daniel (1733–1782) - accessed 2014-09-07 Jonsell, Bengt: Daniel C Solander in the Swedish Biographical Encyclopedia (2003-2006) - accessed 2014-09-07 Solander, Daniel Karl in Nordisk familjebok (second edition, 1917)

== Literature == Pär Ahlberger, "Solander 250: A Journey from the South Seas to the Arctic," in the Swedish Linnaeus Society's Yearbook 2022. Edward Duyker & Per Tingbrand (eds. & trans.), Daniel Solander: Collected Correspondence 1753—1782, Miegunyah/Melbourne University Press, Melbourne, 1995, pp. 466, ISBN 0-522-84636-X [Scandinavian University Press, Oslo, 1995 ISBN 82-00-22454-6] Edward Duyker, Nature's Argonaut: Daniel Solander 1733—1782, Naturalist and Voyager with Cook and Banks, Miegunyah/Melbourne University Press, Melbourne, 1998 et 1999, pp. 380, ISBN 0-522-84753-6 Tidström, Anders (2003). "A Linnaeus Student in the South Seas." Popular History 2003:1, pp. 30-36, with illustrations. ISSN 1102-0822. http://www.popularhistoria.se/artiklar/linnelarjunge-i-soderhavet/. Libris 10233591

== External Links == Wikimedia Commons has media related to Daniel Solander. The Solander Association, with the Solander Farm and Solander Park in Piteå. Daniel Solander on postage stamps.

How certain are we about this?

Information about figures from the Age of Liberty is well-documented through parliamentary records, letters, and archives. However, interpretations of their roles are often influenced by later political affiliations.
tldr: Daniel Solander was a Swedish naturalist who traveled around the world and became the first Swede to visit Australia. He is now known for his discoveries of plants and for his unique box designed for storing books.
lattlast:
Daniel Solander was born in Piteå in 1733 and became a very talented student of the renowned scientist Carl von Linné. He traveled extensively to study nature and plants, both in Sweden and later in other countries.

In 1760, he moved to England where he obtained a position at the British Museum, a large national museum and library. There, he worked on organizing and managing important collections of natural history specimens.

Solander participated in a major circumnavigation voyage with Captain James Cook. During this journey, he became the first highly educated Swede to set foot in Australia in 1770. Many places in Australia and other countries have been named after him to honor his work.

He also invented a special box, called a Solander box, which is used to protect books and documents so that they last longer. Today, there are parks, schools, and roads in Sweden that are named after him.
